questions.text: The uppermost layer of the Earth's surface is called the:
questions.explanation: The crust is the thinnest of all the layers and is about 35 km on the continental masses.

questions.text: The main mineral constituents of the continental mass are:
questions.explanation: The continental crust is called 'sial' (si-silica and al-alumina).

questions.text: The thinnest layer of the Earth is the:
questions.explanation: The crust forms only 1 per cent of the volume of the earth, while 84 per cent consists of the mantle and 15 per cent makes the core.

questions.text: Rocks that are formed when molten magma cools and solidifies are called:
questions.explanation: Igneous rocks are also called primary rocks. They are formed from cooling lava or magma.

questions.text: Small fragments of rocks that are compressed and hardened to form layers are:
questions.explanation: Sediments are transported and deposited by wind and water to form sedimentary rocks like sandstone.

questions.text: The innermost layer of the Earth is the core, which is mainly made of:
questions.explanation: The core is called 'nife' (ni – nickel and fe – ferrous, i.e. iron).

questions.text: The process where one type of rock changes into another under certain conditions is the:
questions.explanation: The rock cycle is a continuous process where igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks transform into one another.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a metamorphic rock?
questions.explanation: Limestone changes into marble under great heat and pressure.

questions.text: The remains of dead plants and animals trapped in layers of rocks are called:
questions.explanation: Fossils are generally found in sedimentary rocks.

questions.text: Igneous and sedimentary rocks can change into metamorphic rocks under great:
questions.explanation: Great heat and pressure are the primary drivers for the formation of metamorphic rocks.
